blurb: A stiff dark-green rosette of upright, tapering leaves ridged with raised pearly tubercles. Tough, slow, and happy to sit on a bright shelf out of harsh sun.

care.light: Bright, indirect light is the sweet spot — an east window or filtered light, which also brings out the best color and banding in the leaves. It takes gentle morning sun but scorches in harsh afternoon glare.
care.water: Soak-and-dry: a good drink, then let the soil dry out fully before the next — it's genuinely drought-tolerant and rots if left damp. Ease right off through winter, when it's resting.
care.soil: A fast-draining cactus/succulent mix — roughly one part coarse sand or perlite to two parts potting soil. Standing wet is what kills it.
care.temperature: Ordinary room warmth, around 60–80°F. It shrugs off brief cool spells but isn't frost-hardy — keep it above ~50°F and out of frost (hardy outdoors only in Zones 9–11).
care.dormancy: Does most of its growing in the milder spring and autumn, with a rest in the hottest weeks; give it a cooler, drier winter too or it puts out thin, weak growth that spoils the rosette's shape.
care.feeding: Very little — a dilute high-potash or specialist cactus feed no more than monthly through the spring-to-autumn growing season, and none while it's resting.
care.potting: Slow and happy snug; repot every 2–3 years into fresh gritty mix in a pot with good drainage, mainly to divide the clump of offsets.
care.propagation: Easiest by twisting off rooted offsets from around the base; leaf cuttings work too, but it rarely comes from seed.
care.bloom: A wiry stalk lifts small tubular pale-pink flowers in loose clusters well above the rosette in late spring to early summer; it doesn't die after flowering.
care.troubles: Overwatering rot is the main danger — leaf spot and fungal trouble follow soggy soil or water left sitting in the rosette. Watch for mealybugs in the leaf axils and scale.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic and safe around cats and dogs (ASPCA-listed) — though no plant is meant to be a snack.
